content.text: The launch team that built and shipped an AI agent carries a level of contextual knowledge about the system that no documentation fully captures. They know which corner of the prompt causes the model to behave unexpectedly, which tool integration has a latency tail, and which alert is a known false positive. When operations transitions to the steady-state SRE team, that knowledge must be systematically transferred — or the SRE team will spend months rediscovering what the launch team learned in the weeks before launch.
What It Is

Day-2 steady-state is the operational mode a system enters after the elevated hypercare period following launch. In hypercare, the launch team owns operations with heightened attention and faster escalation. In steady-state, the SRE and support teams own operations with normal cadences, standard alert thresholds, and documented runbooks. The Day-2 handoff is the structured transition between these two modes.
The handoff is not a single event but a process.

For agentic AI systems, the handoff is more complex than for traditional services because the SRE team must understand not only infrastructure operations but also AI-specific concerns: model quality monitoring, prompt management, hallucination incident response, vector database maintenance, and the retraining or fine-tuning pipeline. These topics have no equivalent in standard SRE training and must be explicitly covered in the handoff knowledge transfer.
Why It Matters

The hypercare period cannot last forever. The launch team's engineers need to return to building the next version of the system. But if the SRE team inherits operational ownership without adequate preparation, the result is a support burden that falls back on the launch engineers as informal escalations, a higher incident rate as SREs encounter unfamiliar failure modes, and longer mean time to resolution as SREs rely on the launch team instead of runbooks.

For AI systems specifically, an SRE team that is not prepared for AI-specific failure modes will treat hallucination incidents as server errors, misinterpret model quality metric changes as infrastructure problems, and escalate to the ML team for issues they could resolve themselves with proper runbooks. This creates unnecessary toil for the ML team and delays in resolving user-facing quality issues.

A well-executed Day-2 handoff produces an SRE team that is confident operating the system independently, a support team that can handle AI-specific user complaints with documented escalation paths, and a launch team that can step away without receiving informal support requests.
Benefits
Clean operational responsibility: after handoff, there is a single team accountable for production incidents, not an ambiguous shared zone.
Sustainable operations: the launch team returns to product development; SREs operate with clear runbooks rather than tribal knowledge.
Faster incident resolution: SREs with AI-specific training resolve model quality incidents directly instead of escalating to the ML team.
Support team effectiveness: documented failure modes and escalation paths allow support to handle the most common AI-specific user complaints without engineering involvement.
Improved runbook quality: the handoff process forces completion of any runbooks that were incomplete at launch, improving documentation for all future operators.
Reduced key-person risk: knowledge transfer spreads operational expertise beyond the original launch engineers.
Compliance and audit: a documented handoff with acceptance criteria provides evidence of operational maturity for security and compliance reviews.
Trade-offs
Handoff takes time: the shadow on-call period and knowledge transfer sessions consume engineering time from both the launch team and the SRE team.
Documentation debt: if runbooks are incomplete at launch, the handoff process forces completion of deferred documentation work that is now critical-path.
Knowledge compression loss: no amount of documentation fully captures the contextual knowledge held by the engineers who built the system. Some knowledge transfer is unavoidable.
SRE team ramp-up cost: SREs learning AI-specific operations for the first time will have a higher incident rate in the first few weeks than a fully trained team.
Premature handoff risk: handing off too early — before the system is stable and the runbooks are complete — creates more SRE toil, not less.
How It Works

The Day-2 handoff has four phases: preparation, shadow operations, formal transfer, and post-handoff support. Each phase has specific deliverables and completion criteria. The phases are sequential and should not be compressed to save time — particularly the shadow on-call period, which is the most effective knowledge transfer mechanism.
Phase 1: Preparation (Weeks 1-2 Post-Launch)

During the first two weeks of hypercare, the launch team documents everything they are learning about how the system behaves in production. Alert runbooks are completed or updated based on real alerts observed in the first week. Model quality monitoring thresholds are calibrated against real production data. The on-call escalation tree is finalized with phone numbers and backup contacts. The AI-specific runbooks (model rollback, prompt restore, vector DB recovery) are validated against real scenarios observed in production.
Phase 2: Shadow On-Call (Week 3)

During the shadow week, one or more SRE engineers join the on-call rotation alongside the primary launch team on-call engineer. The SRE engineer receives all alerts and attends all incidents, but the launch engineer retains primary responsibility. The SRE documents anything they do not understand about the system or the runbooks. At the end of the shadow week, the SRE should be able to operate the system independently for at least 70% of expected incident types without consulting the launch team.
Phase 3: Formal Transfer (Week 4)

The formal transfer is a structured meeting with the launch team, the SRE team, and the SRE manager. The handoff checklist is reviewed item by item. Each runbook is assigned an SRE owner. The on-call rotation is updated in PagerDuty with SRE engineers as primary. The launch team moves to a secondary escalation tier for 30 days, reachable by the SRE team for questions not covered by the runbooks.
Phase 4: Post-Handoff Support (Weeks 5-8)

For 30 days after the formal transfer, the launch team is available as a secondary escalation tier. SREs should be encouraged to resolve incidents using runbooks before escalating. After 30 days, the launch team's on-call availability is removed and they are available only for scheduled consultations. After 60 days, the SRE team is fully independent.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Day-2 handoff timeline from launch through hypercare, shadow on-call, formal transfer, and SRE ownership, with handoff package components and readiness gate criteria

content.text: The diagram shows the 4-week timeline from launch to formal handoff, the components of the handoff package, and the readiness gate criteria that both the SRE team and the support team must pass before the formal transfer is complete.
Mental Model

Think of the Day-2 handoff like the transfer of a newly constructed building from the construction team to the property management team. The construction team knows exactly where every pipe runs, which circuit breaker serves which room, and where the structural quirks are. The property management team will maintain the building for years — they need systematic documentation of all of this, a walkthrough with the construction foreman, and a period where both teams are present before the construction team leaves. A construction team that hands over the keys and walks away on Day-1 leaves the property managers to rediscover everything through breakdowns.

The runbooks are the building manual. The shadow on-call period is the walkthrough. The formal handoff is handing over the keys. The post-handoff support is the construction foreman remaining available by phone for the first 30 days.
What Gets Handed Off
Runbooks
System startup and shutdown procedures.
Scaling runbooks (both scale-up for capacity and scale-down for cost).
Alert response runbooks for every configured alert (not just the most common).
Rollback runbooks: code deployment rollback, model version rollback, prompt configuration restore.
Data recovery runbooks: vector DB snapshot restore, conversation history backup restore.
Cost incident runbook: token spend spike response, budget cap activation.
Security incident runbook: prompt injection response, data exfiltration indicator response.
Hallucination incident runbook: what constitutes a hallucination incident, how to assess severity, when to escalate.
Monitoring Dashboards
SLO dashboard with error budget tracking and burn rate alerts.
Model quality dashboard: eval score trends, hallucination rate, user satisfaction signals.
Token cost dashboard: per-request cost trends, daily budget burn rate.
Infrastructure capacity: CPU, memory, queue depth, autoscaling state.
Security alert dashboard: prompt injection detection counts, anomalous access patterns.
Business metrics: active users, query volume, task completion rate.
AI-Specific Handoff Items
These are the items that have no equivalent in a standard SRE handoff and require explicit transfer for AI systems:
Model version registry: what model version is currently pinned, what the previous version was, and how to change the pin.
Prompt management process: where system prompts are stored, how they are versioned, how to roll back a prompt change.
Eval dataset custody: where the evaluation dataset lives, how to run an eval, what score thresholds mean.
Retraining / fine-tuning pipeline ownership: who schedules fine-tuning runs, what triggers a retraining, how to deploy a new fine-tuned checkpoint.
Vector database maintenance: re-indexing schedule, index health checks, snapshot cadence, and the restore procedure.
Hallucination monitoring thresholds and incident classification criteria.
Cost model: how per-request cost is calculated, what drives cost spikes, and the escalation path for cost overruns.
Known false positives in the alert system that the SRE team should suppress or deprioritize.
SRE Readiness Criteria for AI Services
Standard SRE readiness criteria focus on infrastructure operations. For AI services, additional criteria must be met before the SRE team is considered ready to take primary ownership:
Can explain the difference between an infrastructure incident and a model quality incident, and correctly classify a given alert into one of these categories.
Can execute the model version rollback runbook in a staging environment without assistance, within the target time.
Can execute the prompt configuration restore procedure in a staging environment without assistance.
Can read and interpret the model quality dashboard and knows the thresholds at which to escalate to the ML team.
Has completed a tabletop drill of the hallucination incident response runbook.
Can describe the vector database's role in the system and knows when its behavior might indicate an index health problem.
Knows the escalation path to the ML team: when to call, what information to provide, and what response to expect.
Support Team Enablement

The support team handoff is often the most neglected part of the Day-2 transition. Support engineers interact directly with users experiencing AI-related issues, but they rarely have the technical background to distinguish between a model quality issue, a prompt configuration problem, and a software bug. Without explicit enablement, support teams escalate everything to engineering, creating toil and slow response times for users.
What Support Teams Need
A taxonomy of common AI-specific user complaints with a decision tree for each: is this a usage issue, a known limitation, a quality bug, or a potential safety issue?
Approved user-facing language for common failure scenarios: how to describe a hallucination without alarming users or creating legal liability.
Escalation criteria for AI-specific issues: when does a user complaint warrant immediate engineering escalation versus a standard ticket?
The AI system's known limitations (documented, approved by the product team) so support can set accurate expectations.
A feedback loop to the ML team: a mechanism for reporting patterns in user complaints that might indicate a systemic quality issue.
When to Trigger the Handoff

The handoff should not be triggered by a calendar date alone but by a set of readiness criteria. Triggering the handoff too early creates an SRE team that is not ready and a support team that escalates everything. Triggering too late creates launch team burnout and delays product development.
System stability: fewer than 2 P1 incidents in the most recent 7-day period.
Alert signal quality: fewer than 5 alert false positives per day in the most recent 7-day period.
Runbook completeness: every alert has a response runbook; every AI-specific scenario has a runbook.
SRE readiness: SRE team has completed shadow on-call and passed all readiness criteria.
Support enablement: support team has received training on AI-specific failure modes and escalation paths.
Documentation completeness: all runbooks are reviewed, all dashboards are documented, model registry and prompt registry ownership are assigned.
How to Implement
Handoff Checklist YAML Template

day2_handoff:
  system: "AI Customer Service Agent"
  hypercare_start: "2026-01-15"
  target_handoff_date: "2026-02-12"  # 4 weeks post-launch

  documentation:
    runbooks:
      - name: "system-startup"
        location: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/ai-agent/startup"
        sre_owner: "alice@example.com"
        last_drilled: "2026-01-20"
        status: "complete"
      - name: "model-version-rollback"
        location: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/ai-agent/model-rollback"
        sre_owner: "bob@example.com"
        last_drilled: "2026-01-22"
        status: "complete"
      - name: "hallucination-incident"
        location: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/ai-agent/hallucination"
        sre_owner: "carol@example.com"
        last_drilled: "2026-01-20"
        status: "complete"
      - name: "vector-db-restore"
        location: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/ai-agent/vector-restore"
        sre_owner: "bob@example.com"
        last_drilled: null
        status: "needs_drill"

  knowledge_transfer:
    sessions:
      - topic: "System Architecture & AI Components"
        date: "2026-01-28"
        presenter: "lead-ml-engineer"
        attendees: ["sre-team"]
        status: "scheduled"
      - topic: "Model Quality Monitoring & Thresholds"
        date: "2026-01-29"
        presenter: "ml-engineer"
        attendees: ["sre-team"]
        status: "scheduled"
      - topic: "AI-Specific Incident Response"
        date: "2026-01-30"
        presenter: "lead-engineer"
        attendees: ["sre-team", "support-lead"]
        status: "scheduled"

  shadow_oncall:
    period_start: "2026-02-03"
    period_end: "2026-02-10"
    shadow_engineer: "sre-alice"
    primary_engineer: "launch-bob"

  readiness_gate:
    sre_criteria:
      - "Completed shadow on-call week"
      - "Passed all AI-specific runbook drills in staging"
      - "Can interpret model quality dashboard independently"
      - "Knows ML team escalation path"
    support_criteria:
      - "Received AI failure modes training"
      - "Approved user-facing error messages reviewed"
      - "Escalation path documented and tested"
Runbook Handoff Verification Script

import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import date, timedelta
import sys

REQUIRED_RUNBOOK_NAMES = [
    "system-startup",
    "system-shutdown",
    "scaling",
    "alert-response",
    "deployment-rollback",
    "model-version-rollback",
    "prompt-config-restore",
    "vector-db-restore",
    "hallucination-incident",
    "cost-overrun-response",
    "security-incident",
]

MAX_DRILL_AGE_DAYS = 30

def verify_handoff_checklist(checklist_path: str) -> bool:
    with open(checklist_path) as f:
        checklist = json.load(f)

    runbooks = {r["name"]: r for r in checklist.get("runbooks", [])}
    all_ok = True

    print("=== RUNBOOK HANDOFF VERIFICATION ===")
    for name in REQUIRED_RUNBOOK_NAMES:
        if name not in runbooks:
            print(f"  MISSING: {name}")
            all_ok = False
            continue
        rb = runbooks[name]
        issues = []
        if not rb.get("sre_owner"):
            issues.append("no SRE owner assigned")
        if rb.get("status") != "complete":
            issues.append(f"status={rb.get('status')}")
        if rb.get("last_drilled"):
            last_drilled = date.fromisoformat(rb["last_drilled"])
            age = (date.today() - last_drilled).days
            if age > MAX_DRILL_AGE_DAYS:
                issues.append(f"last drilled {age} days ago (max {MAX_DRILL_AGE_DAYS})")
        else:
            issues.append("never drilled")
        if issues:
            print(f"  ISSUES [{name}]: {"; ".join(issues)}")
            all_ok = False
        else:
            print(f"  OK: {name} (owner: {rb["sre_owner"]})")

    print(""
    if all_ok:
        print("Handoff verification PASSED")
    else:
        print("Handoff verification FAILED: resolve issues before formal transfer")
    return all_ok

if __name__ == "__main__":
    ok = verify_handoff_checklist(sys.argv[1])
    sys.exit(0 if ok else 1)
Examples

A practical shadow on-call session: SRE engineer Alice shadows launch engineer Bob during a weekday on-call shift. At 2:47 PM, an alert fires: model eval score dropped below the warning threshold. Bob explains to Alice what the alert means, shows her the quality dashboard, and walks through the investigation: checking whether the drop is correlated with a deployment, a prompt change, or an unusual input pattern. In this case, the drop is caused by a spike of very short queries that score lower on the eval rubric. No action is needed. Alice documents the pattern in her shadow notes. The runbook does not mention this type of false positive. Bob adds a note to the runbook after the incident.
Real-World Example: Enterprise AI Assistant Handoff

An enterprise software company launched an AI coding assistant for internal developers. The launch team consisted of 3 ML engineers and 2 backend engineers. After a 4-week hypercare period, they handed off to an SRE team of 2. The handoff process revealed three gaps: the vector DB restore runbook had not been drilled (fixed with a staging drill on Week 3), the model quality dashboard had no documented explanation of the eval scoring rubric (ML engineer recorded a 15-minute Loom video explaining it), and the support team had no escalation criteria for hallucinated code suggestions (product manager and ML team defined clear criteria: syntactically invalid code is a P2, semantically dangerous code in a security context is a P1).

The formal handoff meeting took 90 minutes and produced a signed acceptance document. For the first 2 weeks after handoff, the SRE team escalated to the launch team 4 times. All 4 escalations were for situations covered by runbooks that the SRE team had not yet read. The ML engineer conducted a 30-minute runbook review session with the SRE team, and escalations dropped to zero in the following week. By Week 8, the SRE team had independently resolved 3 model quality incidents, 1 vector DB issue, and 2 cost overrun events without launch team involvement.
Common Mistakes
Handoff with incomplete runbooks: handing off with partially-written runbooks forces SREs to improvise under pressure, which is exactly what runbooks are designed to prevent.
SRE team not trained on AI-specific failure modes: an SRE who treats a hallucination incident as a server error will apply the wrong response procedure and escalate incorrectly.
No shadow on-call period: jumping from documentation review directly to primary ownership means the first real incident is the SRE team's first real experience with the system under pressure.
Support team excluded from handoff: support teams that are not explicitly enabled for AI-specific issues create an escalation funnel that flows directly to engineering for every AI complaint.
Launch team remains informally available too long: if the launch team continues answering informal Slack questions indefinitely, the SRE team never fully builds the skills to operate independently.
Runbooks never drilled by the SRE team: an SRE team that has read a runbook but never executed it is in nearly the same position as one that has never seen it.
No formal acceptance criteria: a handoff without documented acceptance criteria is a handoff that never officially completes, leaving ownership ambiguous.
Best Practices
Begin handoff documentation at T+1 week, not at the end of hypercare — early documentation while experiences are fresh produces better runbooks.
Require the SRE team to execute each AI-specific runbook in staging before the shadow on-call period begins, not after.
Record knowledge transfer sessions so that future SRE team members can onboard without needing to repeat the sessions with the launch team.
Assign specific SRE owners to each runbook during the handoff meeting, not as a group — collective ownership is no ownership.
Set a 30-day post-handoff consultation period with explicit terms: the launch team will respond to escalations within 4 business hours, not immediately.
Track escalations to the launch team after handoff as a metric: a declining trend is evidence of SRE team skill development; a flat or rising trend is a signal that more knowledge transfer is needed.
Include the support lead in the formal handoff meeting, not just the SRE team.
Review and update the handoff checklist template after each handoff to incorporate lessons learned.
Performance & Scalability

As an organization matures in AI operations, the Day-2 handoff process should become more efficient. Mature organizations develop standardized handoff templates, SRE onboarding curricula for AI systems, and shared runbook libraries that reduce the per-system documentation burden. The first handoff of an AI system type will always be the hardest; subsequent handoffs of similar systems benefit from the runbooks, templates, and trained SRE team members from the first.
Invest in reusable runbook templates for common AI service patterns — model rollback, prompt restore, vector DB recovery — that can be adapted for new services.
Build an SRE onboarding curriculum for AI systems that covers model quality concepts, embedding databases, and cost management without requiring the launch team to teach it from scratch each time.
Track time-to-independent-operation for each handoff and use it as the primary efficiency metric for the handoff process.
For organizations running many AI services, consider an AI operations guild within the SRE team to centralize expertise.
Tooling & Ecosystem
PagerDuty: for updating the on-call rotation from launch team to SRE at the formal handoff.
Backstage (Spotify): service catalog for documenting service ownership, runbook locations, and SRE contacts.
Confluence / Notion: for maintaining living runbooks that the SRE team can update as they gain operational experience.
Loom / screen recording: for capturing knowledge transfer sessions that future SRE team members can watch independently.
Linear / Jira: for tracking post-handoff action items (runbook gaps, drill completions, support team training).
Datadog / Grafana: ensuring SRE team members have the correct dashboard permissions before the formal handoff.
Comparison with Alternatives

Security & Reliability

The handoff itself introduces security and reliability risks if not managed carefully. During the shadow on-call period, both the launch team and the SRE team have production access, which temporarily increases the number of people with elevated privileges. This period should be time-bounded and access should be removed promptly when the shadow period ends.
Audit production access grants for SRE team members during the handoff period and remove launch team access promptly after formal transfer.

Ensure SRE team members receive security training specific to AI systems before receiving production access: what constitutes a security incident in an AI context, how to handle potential data exfiltration indicators.
Do not transfer ownership of credentials or service accounts — provision new credentials for the SRE team's access rather than sharing the launch team's credentials.
Verify that the SRE team's access is scoped to the minimum required for operations — read-only access to model registry, config store, and vector DB is sufficient for most operational tasks.
Document the handoff in the access control audit log with the formal transfer date so there is a clear record of when operational responsibility changed hands.
Key Takeaways
The Day-2 handoff is a process, not an event — it spans weeks of documentation preparation, shadow on-call, and formal transfer, and cannot be compressed without accepting higher transition risk.

AI systems require SRE training that goes beyond standard infrastructure operations: model quality monitoring, prompt management, hallucination incident response, and vector database maintenance are all AI-specific skills that must be explicitly transferred.

The shadow on-call period is the highest-value component of the handoff — no amount of documentation reading produces the same operational readiness as having been on-call for real incidents alongside an experienced engineer.
Support team enablement is as important as SRE enablement — a support team that cannot handle common AI complaints creates an escalation funnel that flows directly to engineering indefinitely.
Set explicit readiness criteria that must be met before the formal transfer — a checklist with pass/fail items is more reliable than a judgment call made under time pressure.
Begin documenting the handoff package at T+1 week post-launch, while the launch team's production experience is fresh, not at the end of the hypercare period.
Track escalations to the launch team after handoff as a metric of SRE team capability development and intervene with additional knowledge transfer if escalation rates do not decline.
Remove launch team production access promptly after formal transfer to maintain clean security boundaries and prevent the informal support relationship from persisting indefinitely.
